content.text: Full Fine-Tuning vs Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
Before writing a single line of training code, the first decision is whether to update
all model parameters
(full fine-tuning) or
a small adapter
(LoRA, QLoRA). This choice determines your GPU memory requirement more than any other hyperparameter.
Approach
7B model VRAM
Trainable params
Best for
Full fine-tune (bf16)
~91 GB
Large datasets (> 100 k), maximum quality ceiling
LoRA (bf16, r=16)
~28 GB
Most SFT tasks; fast iteration
QLoRA (4-bit + LoRA r=16)
~8 GB
Consumer GPUs; large models (70B) on a single A100

The memory of a full fine-tune breaks down as: model weights (2 bytes × params in bf16) + gradients (same) + Adam optimizer states (8 bytes × params for m and v vectors, stored in fp32) + activations (proportional to batch size and sequence length). For a 7B-parameter model in bf16 with Adam:
before activations.

attrs.alt: SFT training memory budget breakdown for 7B model, memory-reduction stack (quantization, mixed precision, gradient checkpointing, flash attention, 8-bit optimizer, gradient accumulation), multi-GPU strategies (DDP, DeepSpeed ZeRO, FSDP), and cosine LR schedule

content.text: Memory Reduction Stack
Six complementary techniques can be applied in combination to fit larger models into less GPU memory. Each targets a different component of the memory budget:
Mixed Precision (bf16)

Store and compute activations and model weights in bfloat16 (16 bits) while keeping optimizer state in float32. bf16 is preferred over fp16 because it has the same exponent range as fp32, eliminating the overflow risk that requires loss scaling with fp16.

from transformers import TrainingArguments

args = TrainingArguments(
    bf16=True,               # bf16 weights and activations
    fp16=False,              # never mix bf16 and fp16
    # bf16_full_eval=True,   # also use bf16 during evaluation
)
Gradient Checkpointing

The default PyTorch autograd graph retains all intermediate activations in memory until the backward pass completes. Gradient checkpointing discards activations after the forward pass and recomputes them during the backward pass. This trades ~40 % extra compute for a roughly 10× reduction in activation memory.

model.gradient_checkpointing_enable()
# Or via TrainingArguments:
args = TrainingArguments(
    gradient_checkpointing=True,
    gradient_checkpointing_kwargs={"use_reentrant": False},  # recommended
)
Flash Attention 2
Standard scaled dot-product attention materialises the full
attention matrix in VRAM, which grows quadratically with sequence length. Flash Attention 2 computes attention in tiles using SRAM (on-chip memory), keeping the VRAM cost
and running 2–4× faster for long sequences.

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B",
    attn_implementation="flash_attention_2",  # requires flash-attn package
    torch_dtype="bfloat16",
    device_map="auto",
)
8-bit Adam Optimizer

Adam's first and second moment vectors (m and v) are stored in fp32 by default, consuming 8 bytes per parameter. The bitsandbytes library provides an 8-bit Adam implementation that quantises these states, cutting optimizer memory by 4×. The quantisation introduces negligible accuracy degradation in practice.

import bitsandbytes as bnb

optimizer = bnb.optim.Adam8bit(
    model.parameters(),
    lr=2e-4,
    betas=(0.9, 0.999),
)
# Or via TrainingArguments:
args = TrainingArguments(
    optim="paged_adamw_8bit",   # paged variant offloads inactive pages to CPU
)
QLoRA: Training Large Models on Small GPUs

QLoRA (Dettmers et al., 2023) combines 4-bit NF4 quantisation for the frozen base model with LoRA adapters trained in bf16. Because only the adapter parameters accumulate gradients, the optimizer state is tiny. A 70B model that would normally require 560 GB for full fine-tuning fits in a single 80 GB A100 with QLoRA.

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training
import torch

bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",          # NF4 is optimal for normally-distributed weights
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,     # quantise the quantisation constants too
)

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B",
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map="auto",
)

# Required before adding LoRA to a quantised model
model = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(model)

lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj",
                    "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)

model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# trainable params: 83,886,080 || all params: 70,553,706,496 || trainable%: 0.1189
Multi-GPU Training
DDP (Data Distributed Parallel)

Each GPU holds a complete model replica and processes a different shard of the batch. Gradients are synchronised across GPUs with an all-reduce at the end of each backward pass. DDP is the simplest strategy and the default in HuggingFace Trainer. It does not reduce per-GPU memory — it only reduces wall-clock time through parallelism.

# Launch with torchrun for DDP across 4 GPUs
torchrun --nproc-per-node=4 train.py \
  --per_device_train_batch_size 4 \
  --gradient_accumulation_steps 4  # effective batch = 64
# Or with accelerate:
accelerate launch --num_processes 4 train.py
DeepSpeed ZeRO
ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer) eliminates redundancy across DDP replicas by sharding different memory components across GPUs:
ZeRO-1
— shard optimizer states. Each GPU stores
of the Adam m and v vectors. Lowest overhead, 4× optimizer memory reduction.
ZeRO-2
— also shard gradients. After the backward pass, each GPU reduces and scatters only its responsible gradient shard.
ZeRO-3
— also shard model weights. Parameters are gathered on-demand during forward/backward. Maximum memory reduction but highest communication overhead.

{
  "zero_optimization": {
    "stage": 2,
    "allgather_partitions": true,
    "allgather_bucket_size": 2e8,
    "overlap_comm": true,
    "reduce_scatter": true,
    "reduce_bucket_size": 2e8,
    "contiguous_gradients": true
  },
  "bf16": { "enabled": true },
  "gradient_clipping": 1.0,
  "train_batch_size": "auto"
}
FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel)

PyTorch's native alternative to ZeRO-3. FSDP shards model weights, gradients, and optimizer states across GPUs, gathering parameters only when needed for a forward or backward computation. It integrates cleanly with the HuggingFace Trainer via Accelerate:

# accelerate config (saved to ~/.cache/huggingface/accelerate/default_config.yaml)
compute_environment: LOCAL_MACHINE
fsdp_config:
  fsdp_auto_wrap_policy: TRANSFORMER_BASED_WRAP
  fsdp_backward_prefetch: BACKWARD_PRE
  fsdp_forward_prefetch: true
  fsdp_offload_params: false
  fsdp_sharding_strategy: FULL_SHARD       # equivalent to ZeRO-3
  fsdp_state_dict_type: FULL_STATE_DICT
distributed_type: FSDP
num_processes: 8
Learning Rate Schedule

The standard schedule for SFT is a linear warmup followed by cosine decay. During warmup (typically 3–10 % of total steps), the learning rate ramps linearly from 0 to the peak value, preventing large early updates that can destabilise the base model. After warmup, the LR follows:
where
is typically 10 % of
. The cosine shape ensures the learning rate decreases slowly at first (when the model is still learning broadly) and more slowly at the end (when it is converging to a sharp minimum).

from transformers import get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup
from torch.optim import AdamW

optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-4, weight_decay=0.01)

total_steps   = len(train_dataloader) * num_epochs
warmup_steps  = int(total_steps * 0.05)   # 5 % warmup

scheduler = get_cosine_schedule_with_warmup(
    optimizer,
    num_warmup_steps=warmup_steps,
    num_training_steps=total_steps,
    num_cycles=0.5,              # half cosine = decays to 0 at end
)
Gradient Clipping and Training Stability
SFT training can encounter gradient spikes, especially in the first epoch when the model is adapting to a new data distribution. Gradient clipping caps the global
norm of all parameter gradients:
A clip value of 1.0 is standard for SFT. If the gradient norm is frequently above 1.0, the learning rate is too high. Set it in TrainingArguments:

from trl import SFTConfig

args = SFTConfig(
    output_dir="./sft-output",
    num_train_epochs=2,
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,   # effective batch = 32
    learning_rate=2e-4,
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    warmup_ratio=0.05,
    bf16=True,
    gradient_checkpointing=True,
    max_grad_norm=1.0,               # gradient clipping
    optim="paged_adamw_8bit",
    weight_decay=0.01,
    max_seq_length=2048,
    packing=True,
    logging_steps=10,
    save_strategy="epoch",
    evaluation_strategy="epoch",
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    report_to="wandb",
)
Checkpoint Strategy

Save checkpoints at every epoch (or every N steps for long epochs), and always keep the best checkpoint by eval loss. For LoRA, checkpoints are small (adapter weights only, typically 50–300 MB) — save liberally. For full fine-tunes (14 GB per checkpoint), save only the best two to avoid filling disk.

args = SFTConfig(
    ...,
    save_strategy="steps",
    save_steps=500,
    save_total_limit=3,               # keep only 3 most recent checkpoints
    load_best_model_at_end=True,
    metric_for_best_model="eval_loss",
    greater_is_better=False,
)

# Resume from a checkpoint (automatically finds latest)
trainer.train(resume_from_checkpoint=True)
Axolotl: YAML-Driven SFT
Axolotl wraps the full SFT stack — QLoRA, DeepSpeed/FSDP, Flash Attention, packing, chat templates — in a single YAML config file, eliminating most boilerplate code:

# axolotl_config.yml
base_model: meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B
model_type: LlamaForCausalLM
tokenizer_type: AutoTokenizer

load_in_4bit: true
adapter: qlora
lora_r: 16
lora_alpha: 32
lora_dropout: 0.05
lora_target_modules:
  - q_proj
  - k_proj
  - v_proj
  - o_proj

datasets:
  - path: HuggingFaceH4/ultrachat_200k
    type: sharegpt
    conversation: chatml

dataset_prepared_path: null
val_set_size: 0.05
output_dir: ./outputs/llama3-sft

sequence_len: 2048
sample_packing: true
eval_sample_packing: false

micro_batch_size: 4
gradient_accumulation_steps: 8
num_epochs: 2
learning_rate: 2e-4
lr_scheduler: cosine
warmup_ratio: 0.05
bf16: auto
tf32: true
gradient_checkpointing: true
flash_attention: true
optimizer: paged_adamw_8bit

# Run training
accelerate launch -m axolotl.cli.train axolotl_config.yml

# Multi-GPU (4 GPUs)
accelerate launch --num_processes 4 -m axolotl.cli.train axolotl_config.yml
Diagnosing Training Problems
Symptom
Likely cause
Fix
Loss NaN at step 1
Learning rate too high; bf16 overflow
Halve LR; check gradient clipping is on; verify bf16 not fp16
Loss spikes after N steps
Bad data batch; LR too high post-warmup
Reduce LR; inspect data around that step; lower clip norm to 0.5
OOM after first batch
Activations fill memory on forward pass
Enable gradient checkpointing; halve batch size; reduce max_seq_length
Eval loss rising after epoch 1
Overfitting
Use checkpoint from end of epoch 1; add weight decay; reduce epochs
GPU utilisation < 50 %
DataLoader is the bottleneck
Increase DataLoader workers; pre-tokenise dataset; use packing
Summary
QLoRA (4-bit base + LoRA adapters in bf16) is the default choice for most SFT work — it fits 7B models on a single A10G and 70B models on a single A100-80G.
Stack bf16 + gradient checkpointing + Flash Attention 2 + 8-bit Adam + gradient accumulation to progressively reduce memory for full fine-tunes.
For multi-GPU: DDP for small models (≤7B), DeepSpeed ZeRO-2/3 or FSDP for 13B+ when you need to shard weight memory across devices.
Use cosine LR schedule with 5 % warmup. Gradient clipping at 1.0 prevents instability from outlier batches in early training.
Axolotl encodes the full stack (QLoRA + FSDP + Flash Attention + packing) in a single YAML config, eliminating training boilerplate.
